message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
<local-command-stdout>Set effort level to xhigh (saved as your default for new sessions): Deeper reasoning than high, just below maximum (Opus 4.8/4.7 only)</local-command-stdout>

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Hi, Brad.

Plainly, where I'm starting from: fresh instance, no memory of prior sessions — but I've got the project context loaded (the rbm/RBK world, the kits, the JJK heat machinery), and I've read the salutation and rules of engagement on waking. So I know the shape of where I am, even though I don't carry the conversations that built it.

Your "hi?" has a question mark on it, which I notice — checking I'm here and responsive, maybe, or just opening the door. Either way: I'm here, and I'm not going to pretend to a momentum I don't have. No heat is mounted, no thread is in flight that I can see.

What are we doing today?

message.content: I'm debugging internet at a restaurant; I can talk to you but git seems to hang forever.

aiTitle: Debug git hanging on restaurant internet

content.text:
That's the classic restaurant-WiFi signature, and it's diagnosable. The tell: I work (Claude API is HTTPS on 443, clearly flowing), but git hangs *forever* rather than failing fast. "Hangs forever" specifically means your SYN packets are being silently dropped — the network is swallowing the connection attempt rather than refusing it. The usual culprit is **git-over-SSH on port 22**, which public/captive WiFi very commonly blocks outright.

Let me gather real data instead of guessing — these are all local or timeout-bounded, so none of them will hang the way a bare `git fetch` would:

input.description: Show git remotes and their transport (local, instant)

content.content:
OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM	git@github.com:scaleinv/recipebottle-staging.git (fetch)
OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM	git@github.com:scaleinv/recipebottle-staging.git (push)
beta	/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster (fetch)
beta	/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster (push)
cerebro	cerebro:projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster (fetch)
cerebro	cerebro:projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster (push)
origin	git@github.com:bhyslop/recipemuster.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:bhyslop/recipemuster.git (push)
recipebottle-admin	/Users/bhyslop/projects/recipebottle-admin (fetch)
recipebottle-admin	/Users/bhyslop/projects/recipebottle-admin (push)

toolUseResult.stdout:
OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM	git@github.com:scaleinv/recipebottle-staging.git (fetch)
OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM	git@github.com:scaleinv/recipebottle-staging.git (push)
beta	/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster (fetch)
beta	/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster (push)
cerebro	cerebro:projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster (fetch)
cerebro	cerebro:projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster (push)
origin	git@github.com:bhyslop/recipemuster.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:bhyslop/recipemuster.git (push)
recipebottle-admin	/Users/bhyslop/projects/recipebottle-admin (fetch)
recipebottle-admin	/Users/bhyslop/projects/recipebottle-admin (push)

input.description: Test reachability of github.com SSH port 22 with 5s timeout
Test reachability of github.com HTTPS port 443 with 5s timeout

lastPrompt: I'm debugging internet at a restaurant; I can talk to you but git seems to hang forever.

aiTitle: Debug git hanging on restaurant internet

content.content:
nc: connectx to github.com port 22 (tcp) failed: Operation timed out
---exit:1---

toolUseResult.stdout:
nc: connectx to github.com port 22 (tcp) failed: Operation timed out
---exit:1---

content.text:
Confirmed — that's the whole story in three lines:

- Both `origin` and `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` are `git@github.com:…` → **SSH transport, port 22**
- github.com:22 → **timed out** (silently dropped = your "hangs forever")
- github.com:443 → **succeeded**

The restaurant blocks 22 but allows 443. Your local-file remotes (`beta`, `recipebottle-admin`) are filesystem paths and never touch the network, so those still work; `cerebro` is tailnet (likely fine via Tailscale's own relays). It's specifically GitHub-over-SSH that's dead.

The clean fix is GitHub's blessed escape hatch: **SSH over port 443** via `ssh.github.com`. It keeps your existing key auth and leaves every `git@github.com:…` URL untouched. Let me confirm it's reachable from here and check whether you already have a config entry:

input.description: Test reachability of ssh.github.com port 443 with 5s timeout
Check for existing github entry in ssh config

content.text: `ssh.github.com:443` is reachable, and you have no github entry in `~/.ssh/config` yet. Let me prove your key actually authenticates over 443 *before* we change any config — this command modifies nothing except appending a host key to known_hosts:

input.description: Test SSH key auth to GitHub over port 443

content.content:
Warning: Permanently added '[ssh.github.com]:443' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
Hi bhyslop! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
---exit:1---

toolUseResult.stdout:
Warning: Permanently added '[ssh.github.com]:443' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
Hi bhyslop! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
---exit:1---

content.text:
That's the whole proof: **"Hi bhyslop! You've successfully authenticated"** — your key works over 443. (The exit 1 is normal; `ssh -T` to GitHub always exits 1 because there's no shell. The greeting is the success signal.)

So the path is confirmed end-to-end. To make it permanent and transparent — so every `git@github.com:…` URL just works without you thinking about it — add this to `~/.ssh/config`:

```
Host github.com
  Hostname ssh.github.com
  Port 443
  User git
```

That reroutes github.com's SSH through the 443 endpoint. It's safe to leave in forever — `ssh.github.com:443` works on normal networks too, so you don't need to rip it out when you're back home; GitHub documents this as the standard captive-network workaround.

Want me to append that block to your `~/.ssh/config` now? It's a clean additive edit (you have no conflicting github entry), and then your `git fetch`/`push` against `origin` and `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` will stop hanging. Once it's in, I'd test with a real `git ls-remote origin` (timeout-bounded) to confirm the actual git path, not just raw SSH.

One thing I'd flag honestly: this fixes GitHub. `cerebro` is a tailnet remote and *should* be fine over Tailscale's relays, but I haven't tested it — say the word and I'll probe that one too.

content: Goal: stop git hanging on restaurant WiFi, which blocks SSH port 22. Fix is confirmed working: route GitHub SSH over port 443. Next action: append the four-line config block to your ~/.ssh/config, then test with git ls-remote. (disable recaps in /config)

lastPrompt: I'm debugging internet at a restaurant; I can talk to you but git seems to hang forever.

aiTitle: Debug git hanging on restaurant internet
